title: The Dynamical Schinzel-Zassenhaus Conjecture and the Transfinite Diameter of Trees
speaker: Philipp Habegger
abstract: In 2019, Dimitrov proved the Schinzel-Zassenhaus Conjecture. Harry Schmidt and I extended his general strategy to cover some dynamical variants of this conjecture. One common tool in both results is Dubinin's Theorem on the transfinite diameter of hedgehogs, a star-shaped tree in the plane. I will report on joint work in progress with Harry Schmidt. We find new upper bounds for the transfinite diameter of some finite topological trees. Our trees arise from the Hubbard tree of a postcritically finite polynomial and reflect its dynamical properties. As a consequence, we prove new lower bounds for the Call-Silverman or canonical height for a class of postcritically finite polynomials.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Classical height bounds and Schinzel-Zassenhaus motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with absolute logarithmic Weil heights, Lehmer's conjectural reciprocal-degree lower bound, Dobrowolski's near-best known bound, and Dimitrov's pointwise complex-conjugate Schinzel-Zassenhaus theorem as the classical template.

proof_stages.label: Dynamical canonical heights and wandering points
proof_stages.summary: For a polynomial dynamical system over a number field, the speaker defines local and global canonical heights, explains that height zero is equivalent to finite forward orbit, and formulates the dynamical Lehmer problem with Baker's partial results.

proof_stages.label: PCF polynomial theorem and hypotheses
proof_stages.summary: After defining postcritically finite polynomials, the main theorem is stated for a prime-degree centered algebraic-integral PCF polynomial with periodic critical points and an unramifiedness condition: wandering algebraic points have global canonical height at least c/[K(α):K]^2, via a sharper local archimedean max bound of order 1/[K(α):K].

proof_stages.label: Transfinite diameter and Pólya-type rationality criterion
proof_stages.summary: The proof imports potential theory by defining transfinite diameter/capacity, recording that monic filled Julia sets have capacity one, and stating a Pólya/Borel-Dwork rationality theorem for integral power series holomorphic off a compact set of capacity less than one.

proof_stages.label: p-th-root power series reduction to a small tree
proof_stages.summary: From the conjugates of a small-height point the speaker builds a rational product of iterates and then a suitably integral p-th-root power series; if all zeroes and poles lie on a compact tree of transfinite diameter less than one, Pólya rationality forces cancellations that would make the point preperiodic.

proof_stages.label: Hubbard trees and preimage trees
proof_stages.summary: For PCF polynomials, Hubbard trees contain all critical orbits, lie in the filled Julia set, are forward invariant, and their iterated preimages remain trees whose transfinite diameters are computable by the preimage capacity formula and stay below one.

proof_stages.label: Core entropy gap and geometric control
proof_stages.summary: The invariant tree induces a Markov map on edges whose leading eigenvalue is the core entropy; under the periodic-critical hypotheses this entropy is strictly smaller than the degree, giving sub-degree growth of tree diameter and allowing a logarithmic-depth preimage tree to be connected to nearby conjugates without losing the capacity bound.

proof_stages.label: Tree-construction proposition and closing contradiction
proof_stages.summary: The technical proposition asserts that any finite set whose local canonical heights are O(1/|A|) is contained in a tree of transfinite diameter less than one, and inserting this tree into the p-th-root rationality argument yields the desired local lower bound and hence the global polynomial height bound.

examples.name: Basic Weil-height calibrations
examples.note: The speaker uses radicals such as a d-th root of 2 together with roots of unity and 0 to make concrete the normalization and the height-zero exceptional set in the classical setting.
examples.engagement_reason: He calibrates the Weil height by plugging in specific algebraic numbers, computing values such as h(2^(1/d)) = log 2 and h(roots of unity)=h(0)=0.

examples.name: The squaring map t↦t²
examples.note: The map t² illustrates that the canonical local height is log⁺|α|, that the global dynamical height recovers the Weil height, that PCF/Hubbard-tree behavior can degenerate to a point, and that the classical Schinzel-Zassenhaus case fits the dynamical picture.
examples.engagement_reason: He repeatedly works out the squaring map: its iterates give the local canonical height log⁺|α|, recover the usual Weil height, have critical orbit 0→0, filled Julia set the unit disk, and a one-point Hubbard tree.

passes.reason: the monomial squaring map has a fixed critical point and a one-point Hubbard tree, the trivial base case of the construction.
The squaring map is the trivial monomial case with fixed critical point and a Hubbard tree collapsed to one vertex, used as a baseline rather than to refute anything.
the monomial squaring map is maximally regular and has the collapsed one-point critical orbit/Hubbard tree base case
the monomial squaring map has maximally regular unit-disk dynamics and its Hubbard tree collapses to the fixed critical point.

the monomial squaring map has a fixed critical point, a one-point Hubbard tree, and maximally regular disk/circle dynamics

examples.name: The Chebyshev-type polynomial t²−2
examples.note: The map t²−2 illustrates a PCF but not purely periodic critical orbit and later shows why the entropy-gap hypothesis is needed, since its core entropy reaches the degree and the diameter control breaks down.
examples.engagement_reason: He traces the critical orbit for t²−2, identifies its filled Julia set/capacity behavior, and analyzes why it is excluded from the stated theorem and is extremal for the tree-growth parameter.

passes.reason: the Chebyshev map is a highly regular boundary case with core entropy equal to the degree, obstructing the tree estimate beyond the stated hypotheses.

examples.name: Quadratic family t²+c and PCF parameters
examples.note: The family t²+c, with the critical point forced to return after n iterates, illustrates that many PCF parameters are algebraic while generic choices such as c=-1/2 are not PCF.
examples.engagement_reason: He explains how PCF parameters in t²+c are produced by imposing return of the critical point after n steps, yielding explicit polynomial conditions in c, and contrasts this with a non-PCF parameter.

passes.reason: the family t²+c supplies an infinite normal-form parameter family with countably many PCF parameters, not a sporadic or extremal object.

examples.name: Standard capacity computations
examples.note: The unit disk/circle, the unit interval, intervals of length four, and a square are used to calibrate the transfinite diameter and to show that it is easy in some symmetric cases but hard in general.

examples.name: Dubinin hedgehogs in the classical argument
examples.note: Star-shaped hedgehog trees from Dubinin's theorem are mentioned as the classical analogue whose sharp capacity estimate supplies Dimitrov's numerical constant.
examples.engagement_reason: He describes Dubinin’s hedgehogs as the star-shaped trees used in the classical argument and cites Dubinin’s theorem and constant, but does not work through the construction or capacity estimate in detail.
